text: This is a robotic procedure for superior mesenteric artery syndrome, period. This patient is a thin, thirty-one-year-old female who's had six months of pain postprandially, and workup within the vascular service showed compression of the duodenum by her superior mesenteric artery, which you can see here. Both CT angiogram and flow studies showed that the velocities were consistent with obstruction of the duodenum. She's had pain postprandially for about six months with some weight loss. Some of the pain has been relieved with bending forward after eating period.
The surgeon reflects the transverse colon cephalad, revealing the duodenum. The thin mesentery allows easy access to the third and fourth portions of the duodenum.

The surgeon uses monopolar shears to create an aneurotomy in both the jejunum and duodenum, preparing for side-to-side approximation of the loops approximately forty to fifty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. This facilitates the placement of an endoscopic stapler through the openings.

The surgeon uses the da Vinci 45mm stapler, inserted through the enterotomies in the jejunum and duodenum, to perform a side-to-side anastomosis. Stay stitches are visible, aiding in the alignment of the tissue. The stapler is clamped and positioned correctly for the anastomosis.

The surgeon removes the stapler and closes the common aneurotomy with a running 3-0 PDS suture, starting from both ends and tying it in the center. The closure is then reinforced with an over-sewn layer using 3-0 Vicryl suture.
The surgeon places saline over the anastomosis closure and instills air through a nasogastric tube to check for leaks, confirming that no leaks are present.
